subject: Cardiology
title: Pericarditis
preambleMarkdown:
# Pericarditis

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Pericarditis is part of the coronary artery disease spectrum in which atherosclerotic plaque, thrombosis, vasomotor dysfunction, or supply-demand imbalance reduces myocardial perfusion. Exam questions emphasize risk recognition, ECG and biomarker interpretation, initial stabilization, prevention, and choosing medical therapy, PCI, CABG, or rehabilitation in the correct sequence.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pleuritic chest pain improved by leaning forward is classic.
- Diffuse ST elevation and PR depression may appear on ECG.
- Evaluate for tamponade, myocarditis, infection, malignancy, renal failure, and autoimmune disease.
- NSAID plus colchicine is common first-line treatment when no contraindication.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Pericarditis refers to clinically important myocardial ischemia or coronary atherosclerotic disease, ranging from stable plaque burden to acute plaque disruption depending on the specific syndrome.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stable chronic coronary disease.
- Acute coronary syndrome and myocardial infarction.
- Obstructive versus nonobstructive coronary disease.
- Native-vessel disease versus post-revascularization disease.
- Primary prevention, secondary prevention, and post-event rehabilitation states.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Coronary disease becomes more common with age and clusters with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, chronic kidney disease, family history, inflammatory disease, and prior vascular disease. Exams often test risk stratification, secondary prevention, and atypical presentations in older adults, women, diabetes, and kidney disease.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - The clinically important anatomy is the coronary circulation: left main, LAD, circumflex, RCA, dominance pattern, septal perforators, marginal branches, posterior descending artery, and papillary muscle blood supply. Anatomy explains ECG localization, wall-motion abnormalities, mechanical complications, and PCI or CABG planning.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Myocardial oxygen balance depends on coronary blood flow, diastolic perfusion time, oxygen content, heart rate, afterload, wall stress, and contractility. Ischemia develops when supply cannot meet demand, especially during plaque rupture, fixed stenosis, vasospasm, anemia, hypoxemia, tachyarrhythmia, or shock.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Atherosclerotic plaque narrows coronary arteries and can rupture or erode, exposing thrombogenic material and forming platelet-rich thrombus. Downstream ischemia causes electrical instability, contractile dysfunction, biomarker release when necrosis occurs, and remodeling after injury.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Causes include obstructive atherosclerosis, acute plaque rupture, supply-demand mismatch, vasospasm, coronary embolism, spontaneous coronary artery dissection, microvascular dysfunction, anemia, hypoxemia, and tachyarrhythmia. The exact cause determines whether the event is type 1 MI, type 2 MI, nonischemic injury, or chronic coronary disease.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess smoking, diabetes, hypertension, LDL cholesterol, chronic kidney disease, family history of premature ASCVD, obesity, sedentary behavior, inflammatory disease, sleep apnea, prior preeclampsia, early menopause, and prior coronary or vascular disease.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Chest pressure or tightness, dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea, fatigue, syncope, or reduced exercise tolerance.
- Atypical presentations occur in older adults, women, diabetes, kidney disease, and postoperative patients.
- Complications may include arrhythmia, heart failure, cardiogenic shock, or sudden death.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ongoing chest pain with ischemic ECG changes.
- Hypotension, pulmonary edema, syncope, or malignant arrhythmia.
- New murmur or mechanical complication after infarction.
- Chest pain suggesting aortic dissection before antithrombotics.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Important alternatives include aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism, pericarditis, myocarditis, pneumothorax, pneumonia, esophageal spasm, reflux, biliary disease, panic symptoms, costochondritis, and nonischemic myocardial injury.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then obtain ECG and focused history.
- Use serial troponins when ACS is possible.
- Use risk scores, stress imaging, coronary CTA, or invasive angiography according to acuity and pretest probability.
- Evaluate lipids, diabetes, kidney function, anemia, and other risk modifiers.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use ECG, serial troponins, renal function, electrolytes, CBC, lipid and diabetes assessment, chest imaging when indicated, echocardiography for wall motion or complications, stress testing or coronary CTA for selected stable patients, and invasive angiography for high-risk or occlusion syndromes.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Stabilize unstable patients and activate emergency pathways when ACS is suspected.
- Use antiplatelet, anticoagulant, anti-ischemic, lipid-lowering, and blood-pressure therapies according to syndrome and contraindications.
- Select PCI or CABG based on anatomy, acuity, diabetes, left main or multivessel disease, surgical risk, and patient goals.
- Refer to cardiac rehabilitation and arrange long-term secondary prevention.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Friction rub is highly specific when heard.
- Electrical alternans suggests large effusion/tamponade.
- Tamponade causes hypotension, JVP elevation, and muffled heart sounds.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: lib.md import start -->
Source: Core clinical source notes. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Acute Pericarditis

_Source lines: 2703-2921._

```text
Acute Pericarditis
A. General Characteristics
1. Inflammation of the pericardial sac-may be an isolated finding or
part of an underlying disorder or generalized disease

## 2. Causes

a. Idiopathic (probably postviral): Most cases of idiopathic
pericarditis are presumed to be postviral, usually preceded by a
recent flu-like illness or by upper respiratory or GI symptoms
b. Infectious: Viral (e.g., Coxsackievirus, echovirus, adenovirus,
EBV, influenza, HIV, hepatitis A or B), bacterial (tuberculosis),
fungal, toxoplasmosis
c. Acute MI (first 24 hours after MI)
d. Uremia
e. Collagen vascular diseases (e.g., SLE, scleroderma, rheumatoid
arthritis, sarcoidosis)
f. Neoplasm-especially Hodgkin lymphoma, breast, and lung
cancers
g. Drug-induced lupus syndrome (procainamide, hydralazine)
h. After MI: (Dressler syndrome)-usually weeks to months after MI

i. After surgery-postpericardiotomy syndrome
j. Amyloidosis
k. Radiation
l. Trauma
3. The majority of patients recover within 1 to 3 weeks. A minority of
patients have a prolonged course or recurrent symptoms

## 4. Complications

a. Pericardial effusion
b. Cardiac tamponade-can occur in up to 15% of patients; close
observation is important
Quick HIT
Diagnostic criteria of acute pericarditis (need at least 2/4):
- Classic pleuritic chest pain with positional component
- Pericardial friction rub
- ECG changes-diffuse ST elevations; PR depression, PR
elevation in aVR
- Pericardial effusion (seen on CXR or echocardiogram)
B. Clinical Features
1. Chest pain (most common finding)
a. Often severe and pleuritic
b. Often localized to the retrosternal and left precordial regions
and radiates to the trapezius ridge and neck
c. Pain is positional: It is aggravated by lying supine, coughing,
swallowing, and deep inspiration. Pain is relieved by sitting up
and leaning forward
d. Pain is not always present, depending on the cause (e.g., usually
absent in rheumatoid pericarditis)
2. Fever and leukocytosis may be present
3. Patient may give symptoms of preceding viral illness such as a
nonproductive cough or diarrhea
4. Pericardial friction rub
a. Not always present, but it is very specific for pericarditis
b. Caused by friction between visceral and parietal pericardial
surfaces
c. Scratching, high-pitched sound with up to three components.

Patients may have any or all of the three components:
- Atrial systole (presystolic)
- Ventricular systole (loudest and most frequently heard)
- Early diastole
g. Heard best during expiration with patient sitting up and with

stethoscope placed firmly against the chest
h. Friction rub may come and go over a period of several hours,
and can vary greatly in intensity
C. Diagnosis
1. ECG shows four changes in sequence
a. Diffuse ST elevation and PR depression, with PR elevation in
lead aVR
b. ST segment returns to normal-typically around 1 week
c. T-wave inverts-does not occur in all patients
d. T wave returns to normal
2. Echocardiogram if pericarditis with effusion is suspected
D. Treatment
1. Most cases are self-limited and resolve in 2 to 6 weeks.

2. Treat the underlying cause if known.

3. First-line therapy is high-dose NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen,
naproxen, or indomethacin). Colchicine is recommended as
adjunctive therapy in addition to NSAIDs.

4. Glucocorticoids may be tried if pain does not respond to NSAIDs,
but should be avoided if at all possible, as they are associated with
a high rate of recurrent pericarditis.

5. Relatively uncomplicated cases can be treated as an outpatient.

However, patients with more worrisome symptoms such as fever
and leukocytosis and patients with worrisome features such as
pericardial effusion should be hospitalized.

Constrictive Pericarditis
A. General Characteristics
1. Fibrous scarring of the pericardium leads to rigidity and thickening of
the pericardium, with obliteration of the pericardial cavity.

## 2. Pathophysiology

a. A fibrotic, rigid pericardium restricts the diastolic filling of the
heart.

b. Ventricular filling is unimpeded during early diastole because
intracardiac volume has not yet reached the limit defined by the
stiff pericardium.

c. When intracardiac volume reaches the limit set by the
noncompliant pericardium, ventricular filling is halted abruptly.

(In contrast, ventricular filling is impeded throughout diastole
in cardiac tamponade.)

## 3. Causes

a. In most patients, the cause is never identified and is idiopathic
or related to a previous viral infection.

b. Other causes include recurrent pericarditis, uremia, radiation
therapy, tuberculosis, chronic pericardial effusion, tumor
invasion, connective tissue disorders, and prior surgery
involving the pericardium.

Quick HIT
Diastolic Dysfunction in Constrictive Pericarditis
Early diastole: Rapid filling
Late diastole: Halted filling
Quick HIT
If a patient has clinical signs of cirrhosis (ascites, hepatomegaly)
and distended neck veins, perform tests to rule out constrictive
pericarditis.

B. Clinical Features
1. Patients appear very ill
2. Patients with constrictive pericarditis typically present in one of the
two ways:
a. With symptoms characteristic of fluid overload such as edema,
ascites, and pleural effusions.

b. With symptoms related to the diminished cardiac output such as
dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, and
cachexia.

c. Patient can present with a combination of both of these
findings.

3. Signs include:
a. JVD-most prominent physical finding; central venous pressure
(CVP) is elevated and displays prominent x and y descents
b. Kussmaul sign-JVP (venous pressure) paradoxically increases
during inspiration
c. Pericardial knock-corresponding to the abrupt cessation of
ventricular filling
d. Ascites
e. Dependent edema
4. May be dificult to distinguish from restrictive cardiomyopathy-
may require echo or cardiac catheterization to distinguish these
entities

Quick HIT
Often, constrictive pericarditis progresses to worsening cardiac
output and to hepatic and/or renal failure. Surgical treatment
(pericardiectomy) is indicated in many cases, but has high risk of
morbidity and mortality.

Quick HIT
Echocardiogram is the imaging study of choice for diagnosis of
pericardial effusion and cardiac tamponade physiology (has a high
sensitivity).

C. Diagnosis

## 1. ECG

a. Nonspecific changes such as low QRS voltages, generalized T-
wave flattening or inversion, left atrial abnormalities.

b. AFib is more often seen in advanced disease but overall occurs
in fewer than half of all patients.

## 2. Echocardiogram

a. Increased pericardial thickness is seen in about half of all
patients.

b. A sharp halt in ventricular diastolic filling and atrial
enlargement can also be seen.

3. CT scan and MRI may also show pericardial thickening and
calcifications, and can aid greatly in the diagnosis.

4. Cardiac catheterization
a. Elevated and equal diastolic pressures in all chambers.

b. Ventricular pressure tracing shows a rapid y descent, which has
been described as a dip and plateau or a "square root sign."

D. Treatment
1. Treat the underlying condition
2. Diuretics may be extremely helpful in treating fluid overload
symptoms
3. Surgical pericardiectomy
Quick HIT
Pericardial effusion is important clinically when it develops rapidly
because it may lead to cardiac tamponade.
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

### Source Excerpt: Pericardial Disease

_Source lines: 5142-5474._

```text
VIII. PERICARDIAL DISEASE
The pericardium is composed of a thin visceral layer adjacent to the epicardium and a
loose parietal layer normally separated by approximately 15 to 50 mL of fluid. The pa-
rietal pericardium is composed of a dense collagen layer (approximately 1 mm thick)
C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	61

Cardiovascular Emergencies
with very few elastic fibers. The parietal pericardium creates a minimally distensible
sac that encases the heart.

A. Acute pericarditis
1. Discussion. Pericarditis occurs when inflammation develops within the peri-
cardium. Specific causes are listed in Table 2-6.

a. Postcardiac injury pericarditis often involves blood in the pericardial space.

i. Post-MI pericarditis. Patients with post-MI pericarditis can develop one
of two syndromes:
a) Acute fibrinous pericarditis develops in 20% of patients with acute
transmural infarctions within days of the infarction. This type of
pericarditis is usually of short duration.

b) Dressler syndrome is less prevalent and may result from an autoim-
mune reaction that occurs when antimyocardial antibodies are pro-
duced following the infarction.

ii. Postpericardiotomy syndrome occurs after cardiac surgery.

iii. Posttraumatic pericarditis may be due to blunt or penetrating injury
and is also thought to represent an autoimmune phenomenon due to
circulating autoantibodies elicited after cardiac injury.

b. Uremic pericarditis occurs in up to one-third of patients with end-stage
renal disease and is most frequently seen in those on hemodialysis. Patients
are often asymptomatic with serosanguineous effusions.

c. Idiopathic pericarditis is most common and frequently follows an upper
respiratory infection.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
i. Chest pain is typically severe and retrosternal, and it worsens with
inspiration and when the patient is in the supine position. It is

Postpericardiotomy 	Echovirus infection 	Hydralazine
Posttraumatic 	HIV infection 	Isoniazid
Uremic 	Epstein-Barr virus infection 	Rheumatic fever
Idiopathic 	Bacterial 	Kawasaki syndrome
Malignancy 	Mycobacterium tuberculosis
infection
Collagen vascular disease
Leukemia 	#-Hemolytic Streptococcus
infection
Rheumatoid arthritis
Lymphoma 	Streptococcus pneumoniae
infection
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Metastatic carcinoma 	Syphilis 	Scleroderma
Metastatic melanoma 	Lyme disease 	Dressler syndrome
Familial 	Fungal
Radiation-induced 	Histoplasmosis
Cholesterol pericarditis 	Protozoal
Myxedema 	Chagas disease
Toxoplasmosis
62 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
characteristically referred to the back and trapezius ridge. Pain is usu-
ally improved by sitting forward.

ii. Constitutional symptoms such as fever and malaise are present as well
as dyspnea and possibly dysphagia due to associated irritation of the
distal esophagus.

b. Physical examination may reveal the hallmark pericardial friction rub,
described as a scratchy, leathery sound, with three components resulting
from movement of the heart within the inflamed pericardium.

3. Differential diagnoses. Patients of all ages present to the ED with complaints
of chest pain. The diagnosis of pericarditis must be differentiated from other
causes of chest pain (see I.C).

## 4. Evaluation

a. Electrocardiography (Figure 2-9)
i. ST elevations in many leads are a classic finding that probably rep-
resents associated subepicardial inflammation. Downsloping PR-
segment depression accompanies the ST elevation in the early stages
of the disease and is specific for pericarditis. After several days,
T-wave inversion is seen following normalization of the ST-segment
elevation.

ii. Differentiating these changes from benign early repolarization may be
difficult. The PR depression is usually not present with benign early
repolarization.

iii. ECG changes usually resolve, but in a few patients, they may persist for
years.

b. Radiography. A chest radiograph may show an enlarged cardiac silhouette
resulting from an associated effusion, but usually the chest radiograph is
unremarkable.

c. Echocardiography is useful for demonstrating a pericardial effusion and
excluding tamponade.

d. Laboratory tests. The white blood cell (WBC) count, sedimentation rate,
and serum cardiac enzyme level may all be elevated.

5. Therapy consists of bed rest until the fever and pain subside. Anti-inflamma-
tory agents are useful in controlling the pain, and specific therapy should be
directed toward the underlying cause of the pericarditis, if it is known.

6. Disposition. Hospitalization is indicated for patients with intractable pain and
for those in whom the diagnosis of MI or ischemia cannot be excluded. Some
Quick 	HIT
Friction rubs tend to be in-
termittent over time and may
change in intensity with po-
sition-they are heard best
when the patient is leaning
forward.

2-9 	Electrocardiogram from a 38-year-old man with fever and chest pain caused by acute peri-
carditis. Diffuse ST-segment elevations are evident and PR-segment depression is noted.

C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	63

Cardiovascular Emergencies
authors recommend hospitalization of patients with moderate to large effusions
to observe for signs of tamponade.

B. Pericardial effusion
1. Discussion. Effusion within the pericardial sac develops in response to inflam-
mation or injury. Most commonly, an exudate, the fluid can also represent
blood from an injury or a leaking thoracic dissection of the aorta. Causes are
the same as those of pericarditis (see Table 2-6).

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms may be the same as in pericarditis, but most patients with small
effusions are asymptomatic. Anxiety, dyspnea, or fatigue in patients with
pericardial effusion may be early signs of tamponade.

b. Physical examination findings include decreased heart sounds with or
without a friction rub.

3. Differential diagnosis. Dilated cardiomyopathy may be difficult to distinguish
from pericardial effusion on a chest radiograph. Differentiating a stable pericar-
dial effusion from an evolving process that might result in cardiac tamponade
is difficult and may require close observation in the hospital.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Electrocardiography. An ECG may reveal low QRS voltage throughout or
an alternating QRS amplitude, referred to as electrical alternans, caused by
the swinging of the heart beat to beat within the pericardial effusion.

b. Radiography. A radiograph may show an enlarged cardiac silhouette with a
"water bottle" appearance. Lucent epicardial fat lines may be visible when
displaced into the cardiac silhouette due to the fluid density surrounding
the heart.

c. Bedside echocardiography is very sensitive and specific for demonstrating
an effusion and excluding tamponade.

5. Therapy. Emergent treatment may include pericardiocentesis and should be
performed if symptoms of cardiac tamponade are present. Treatment of under-
lying conditions should be initiated in the ED.

6. Disposition. Patients with newly diagnosed pericardial effusions should be ad-
mitted to the hospital for close observation. Signs or symptoms of an enlarging
effusion mandate admission to an ICU.

C. Cardiac tamponade
1. Discussion. Tamponade can occur when the effusion surrounding the heart
exerts pressure sufficient enough to impair diastolic filling of the ventricles.

Cardiac tamponade is usually fatal if untreated.

a. Factors contributing to the development of tamponade include the rate of
fluid accumulation, the mass of the ventricular muscle, and the total intra-
vascular blood volume.

b. Causes. In patients who are seen in the ED, tamponade is usually a compli-
cation of trauma. However, other causes of tamponade include malignant
effusions, idiopathic pericarditis, and uremia. Patients with acute pericar-
ditis are at risk for cardiac tamponade if treated with anticoagulants.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms include dyspnea, fatigue, possibly chest pain, and other symp-
toms associated with the various causes of pericarditis. Patients may be
moribund due to hemodynamic compromise.

b. Physical examination findings
i. Beck triad of neck vein distention, hypotension, and muffled heart
sounds is classic.

ii. Pulsus paradoxus, which is a decrease in arterial systolic pressure with
inspiration that is greater than normal (i.e., greater than 10 mm Hg), is
an important finding but is not pathognomonic.

iii. Kussmaul sign (i.e., increased jugular venous distention with inspira-
tion) is rare in tamponade.

iv. Examination of the lungs often reveals normal breath sounds, and
peripheral edema is usually absent.

Quick 	HIT
Only a minority of patients
with cardiac tamponade will
display Beck triad as it is a
late finding.

Quick 	HIT
The paradox in pulsus
paradoxus is that, on clinical
examination, one can detect
beats on cardiac ausculta-
tion during inspiration that
cannot be palpated at the
radial pulse.

64 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
3. Differential diagnoses. Tamponade must be distinguished from constrictive
pericarditis and restrictive cardiomyopathy; however, emergently, tamponade
must be assumed to be present in patients with hypotension and jugular
venous distention.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Electrocardiography. Electrical alternans is often noted.

b. Radiography. Cardiomegaly without evidence of pulmonary vascular con-
gestion may be seen.

c. Bedside echocardiography can confirm the diagnosis and will reveal an
effusion with a hyperdynamic heart that demonstrates diastolic collapse of
the right ventricle and atrium.

5. Therapy entails immediate pericardiocentesis; removal of as little as 10 mL of
fluid can be lifesaving. Volume expansion with intravenous fluids and inotro-
pic medications such as dobutamine may improve the stroke volume and car-
diac output in patients in cardiogenic shock when combined with therapeutic
pericardiocentesis.

6. Disposition. Patients with traumatic cardiac tamponade may require emergent
surgery. Patients who are not treated surgically require admission to an ICU for
close observation and definitive treatment.

D. Constrictive pericarditis
1. Discussion. Constrictive pericarditis occurs when diffuse thickening and
scarring with fibrinous adhesions develops within the pericardium following
acute pericarditis, leading to impaired ventricular filling. Ventricular filling is
unimpeded during early diastole but abruptly reduced when the elastic limit
of the pericardium is reached. Causes are the same as those of pericarditis
(see Table 2-6). Once caused predominantly by tuberculosis, chronic constric-
tive pericarditis now more commonly follows uremic pericarditis, postcardiac
injury pericarditis, and viral pericarditis.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms include dyspnea on exertion, mild orthopnea, fatigue, and weak-
ness. Complaints referable to right-sided heart failure may predominate.

Frank pulmonary edema does not occur.

b. Physical examination findings
i. Jugular venous distention with Kussmaul sign may be seen. A paradoxi-
cal pulse is occasionally found.

ii. Cardiac examination may reveal a diastolic shock on palpation and a
pericardial knock on auscultation.

iii. Hepatomegaly with hepatic tenderness and ascites, often more promi-
nent than peripheral edema, is common.

iv. A pericardial friction rub is not usually found.

3. Differential diagnoses. Constrictive pericarditis is often confused with CHF
and may mimic tamponade, restrictive cardiomyopathy, or tricuspid and
pulmonic valvular disease. The diagnosis should be suspected in anyone with
evidence of elevated venous pressure without prior myocardial dysfunction or
in anyone with a clinical picture of hepatic cirrhosis accompanied by distended
neck veins.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Electrocardiography. An ECG may show low QRS amplitude and atrial
arrhythmias.

b. Radiography. A chest radiograph reveals pericardial calcification (best seen
on the lateral view) in 50% of patients. The cardiac silhouette is frequently
normal in size, but some degree of cardiomegaly may be present.

c. Bedside echocardiography is limited in its diagnostic utility but useful in
excluding tamponade.

5. Therapy. Emergent treatment includes fluid restriction, diuretics, and, possibly,
inotropic agents. Mild cases may be followed with medical therapy alone;
however, pericardiectomy is the definitive treatment in patients with elevated
central venous pressure.

C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	65

Cardiovascular Emergencies
6. Disposition. Patients with evidence of decompensation due to decreased car-
diac output or CHF require admission for improved medical management.

Patients in whom the diagnosis is suspected require cardiac catheterization be-
cause constrictive pericarditis is a potentially treatable disease.
```
